request.question: What actor has appeared in the films "Hamlet", "The Woodlanders" and "The Holiday"?

title: Peter Ellis (actor)
Christopher Fairbank
The Holiday
Cesco Baseggio
Rufus Sewell
John Barrymore
Franz Matter
Skylar Astin
Gregory Peck
Alan Cumming
sentences: Peter Ellis (born 30 May 1936) is an English actor.
Peter has worked extensively in theatre and television both as an actor and director.He spent four seasons with the RSC.
Three years with the Old Vic Co including playing Benvolio in Zeffirelli's "Romeo and Juliet".
Hotspur in "Henry IV" and later Rosencrantz in a world tour of "Hamlet" starring Derek Jacobi.
He also played Guildenstern in "Hamlet" on the site of the new Globe Theatre.
Five years at the Crucible Sheffield.
In the West End he appeared in "The Tulip Tree" and in Ray Cooney's "Funny Money".
He is perhaps best known for playing the role of Chief Superintendent Charles Brownlow in the long running ITV drama "The Bill".
Has also appeared as a semi-regular in "Emmerdale Farm" and "Coronation Street".
Other series include "Sam" "Lyttons Diary", "Edward and Mrs Simpson", "The XYY Man", "Nolan", "The Les Dawson Show", Victoria Wood's play "Talent" for Granada and "Acorn Antiques" for BBC TV.
He appeared in the David Mercer television play "In Two Minds" (1967), a work which was directed by Ken Loach.
Christopher "Chris" Fairbank (born 4 October 1953) is an English film, stage and television actor, perhaps best known for his role as Moxey in the hit comedy-drama series "Auf Wiedersehen, Pet".
Born in Hertfordshire, he first developed an interest in acting while appearing in a play at his primary school in Clavering, Essex.
He has numerous television credits to his name, including "Sapphire and Steel", "The Professionals", and "The Scarlet Pimpernel".
He provided voice talent for both of the Wallace and Gromit feature-length films, "Curse of the Were-Rabbit" and "Flushed Away".
Fairbank also appeared as one of the pair of muggers who rob an out-of-town family, heralding the first appearance of the Batman in Tim Burton's 1989 film.

Fairbank also had roles as Mactilburgh the scientist in the film "The Fifth Element," the prisoner Murphy in "Alien 3", and the Player Queen in the Franco Zeffirelli version of "Hamlet", opposite Mel Gibson.
He appeared in the "Goal!
" trilogy as a Newcastle United fan.
The Holiday is a 2006 American romantic comedy film written, produced and directed by Nancy Meyers.

Co-produced by Bruce A. Block, it was filmed in both California and England, and stars Cameron Diaz and Kate Winslet as Amanda and Iris, two lovelorn women from opposite sides of the Atlantic Ocean, who temporarily exchange homes to escape heartbreak during the holiday season.
Jude Law and Jack Black were cast as the film's leading men Graham and Miles, with Eli Wallach, Shannyn Sossamon, Edward Burns and Rufus Sewell playing key supporting roles.
Francesco "Cesco" Baseggio (1897–1971) was an Italian stage, film and television actor.
He was born in Venice, and was identified with Venetian roles during his film career.
He appeared in a mixture of serious, dramatic films, as well as comedies such as "The Brambilla Family Go on Holiday" (1941).
On the stage he frequently appeared in plays by Carlo Goldoni.
Rufus Frederik Sewell ( ; born 29 October 1967) is an English actor.

In film, he has appeared in Kenneth Branagh's rendition of "Hamlet" (1996) playing Fortinbras, "The Woodlanders", "Dangerous Beauty", "Dark City", "A Knight's Tale", "The Illusionist", "Tristan and Isolde", and "Martha, Meet Frank, Daniel and Laurence".

On television, he starred as "Obergruppenführer" John Smith in "The Man in the High Castle", an Italian detective in the BBC's television series "Zen" (2011) and also appeared in the mini-series "The Pillars of the Earth" (2010).
In 1993 he played the hero, Will Ladislaw, in the BBC's adaptation of George Eliot's "Middlemarch".
In 2003, he appeared in the lead role in "".
He starred in the CBS drama "Eleventh Hour", which was cancelled in April 2009.
On stage, he originated the role of Septimus Hodge in Tom Stoppard's "Arcadia" and the role of Jan in Stoppard's "Rock 'n' Roll"; the latter earned him an Olivier Award and a Tony Award nomination.
John Barrymore (born John Sidney Blyth; February 14 or 15, 1882 – May 29, 1942) was an American actor on stage, screen and radio.

A member of the Drew and Barrymore theatrical families, he initially tried to avoid the stage, and briefly attempted a career as an artist, but appeared on stage together with his father Maurice in 1900, and then his sister Ethel the following year.

He began his career in 1903 and first gained attention as a stage actor in light comedy, then high drama, culminating in productions of "Justice" (1916), "Richard III" (1920) and "Hamlet" (1922); his portrayal of Hamlet led to him being called the "greatest living American tragedian".
Franz Matter (1931–1999) was a Swiss actor and film director.
A noted stage actor, Matter also appeared in a number of films including the 1958 literary adaptation "The Cheese Factory in the Hamlet".
He also worked as an assistant director, and directed the 1961 film "Rosen auf Pump".
Skylar Astin Lipstein (born September 23, 1987), known professionally as Skylar Astin, is an American actor and singer.
He became known for portraying Jesse Swanson in the musical films "Pitch Perfect" (2012) and "Pitch Perfect 2" (2015).

He was also in the original cast of the Broadway musical "Spring Awakening", and has since appeared in films such as "Hamlet 2" (2008), "Taking Woodstock" (2009), "Cavemen" (2013), and "21 & Over" (2013).
Eldred Gregory Peck (April 5, 1916 – June 12, 2003) was an American actor who was one of the most popular film stars from the 1940s to the 1960s.
Peck continued to play major film roles until the late 1980s.
His performance as Atticus Finch in the 1962 film "To Kill a Mockingbird" earned him the Academy Award for Best Actor.
He had also been nominated for an Oscar for the same category for "The Keys of the Kingdom" (1944), "The Yearling" (1946), "Gentleman's Agreement" (1947) and "Twelve O'Clock High" (1949).

Other notable films he appeared in include "Spellbound" (1945), "Roman Holiday" (1953), "Moby Dick" (1956, and its 1998 miniseries), "Pork Chop Hill" (1959), "The Guns of Navarone" (1961), "Cape Fear" (1962, and its 1991 remake), "How the West Was Won" (1962), "The Omen" (1976) and "The Boys from Brazil" (1978).
Alan Cumming, OBE (born 27 January 1965), is a Scottish-American actor, singer/performer, author, and activist who has appeared in numerous films, television shows, and plays.

His London stage appearances include "Hamlet", the Maniac in "Accidental Death of an Anarchist" (for which he received an Olivier Award), the lead in "Bent", and the National Theatre of Scotland's "The Bacchae".
On Broadway, he has appeared in "The Threepenny Opera", as the master of ceremonies in "Cabaret" (for which he won a Tony Award), "Design for Living" and a one-man adaptation of "Macbeth".
His best-known film roles include his performances in "Emma", "GoldenEye", the "Spy Kids" trilogy, "Son of the Mask," and "X2".
Cumming also introduces "Masterpiece Mystery!
" for PBS and appeared on "The Good Wife", for which he has been nominated for three Primetime Emmy Awards, two Screen Actors Guild Awards, two Golden Globe Awards and a Satellite Award.
A filming of his Las Vegas cabaret show, "Alan Cumming Sings Sappy Songs", aired on PBS stations in November 2016.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: What actor has appeared in the films "Hamlet", "The Woodlanders" and "The Holiday"?
hop1_docs: Borthwood | Borthwood is a hamlet on the Isle of Wight, adjacent to Borthwood Copse, the National Trust woodland.  Borthwood includes some holiday cottages and a pet kennel.  At the 2011 Census the Post Office specified that the population of the hamlet was included in the civil parish of Newchurch, Isle of Wight.

Andrzej Fedorowicz (actor) | Andrzej Fedorowicz (born 21 January 1942) is a Polish actor.  He has made over 10 appearances in film.  He starred in the 1978 comedy film "What Will You Do When You Catch Me? ".

Creagen Dow | Creagen Dow (born May 1, 1991) is an American/Canadian actor, writer, producer who had a recurring role as Jeremiah Trottman on the Nickelodeon series "Zoey 101".  He has also guest starred on "The Big Bang Theory", "Rizzoli & Isles", "Hannah Montana", "Castle", "Entourage", "Hot in Cleveland" and "", to name a few.  Creagen received a Best Supporting Actor award for his role in the feature film "Hamlet's Ghost" and also appeared alongside Reese Witherspoon and Vince Vaughn in "Four Christmases", with Sean Astin in "Amazing Love" and provided the voice of "Mullet Boy" in the film "The Ant Bully".  Creagen can also be seen in various television commercials including the GEICO Horror Movie - It's What You Do campaign.

Rufus Sewell | Rufus Frederik Sewell ( ; born 29 October 1967) is an English actor.  In film, he has appeared in Kenneth Branagh's rendition of "Hamlet" (1996) playing Fortinbras, "The Woodlanders", "Dangerous Beauty", "Dark City", "A Knight's Tale", "The Illusionist", "Tristan and Isolde", and "Martha, Meet Frank, Daniel and Laurence".  On television, he starred as "Obergruppenführer" John Smith in "The Man in the High Castle", an Italian detective in the BBC's television series "Zen" (2011) and also appeared in the mini-series "The Pillars of the Earth" (2010).  In 1993 he played the hero, Will Ladislaw, in the BBC's adaptation of George Eliot's "Middlemarch".  In 2003, he appeared in the lead role in "".  He starred in the CBS drama "Eleventh Hour", which was cancelled in April 2009.  On stage, he originated the role of Septimus Hodge in Tom Stoppard's "Arcadia" and the role of Jan in Stoppard's "Rock 'n' Roll"; the latter earned him an Olivier Award and a Tony Award nomination.

Linda Regan | Linda Regan (born 1949) is a British author and actress, who has appeared in television and film.  She is best known for her role as Yellowcoat April in the British holiday camp sitcom "Hi-de-Hi! ".  She is married to the actor Brian Murphy.

Mike Estime | Mike Estime is an American actor and comedian.  He has made appearances in the film "Last Holiday" (2006) and the sitcom "Talk to Me" (2000) as well as the hit television series "Everybody Hates Chris" as Risky, a street entrepreneur, and on the BET comedy show "Comic View".

Frank Campeau | Frank Campeau (December 14, 1864 – November 5, 1943) was an American actor.  He appeared in 93 films between 1911 and 1940 and made many appearances in films starring Douglas Fairbanks.  He was born in Detroit, Michigan and died in Woodland Hills, Los Angeles.
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
1) Evidence found:
- Rufus Sewell: passage [4] — "Rufus Frederik Sewell (born 29 October 1967)" and 'appeared in Kenneth Branagh's rendition of "Hamlet" (1996)' and '"The Woodlanders"' (passage [4]).
- No passage explicitly mentions "The Holiday". Passage [6] shows "Last Holiday (2006)" (Mike Estime) which is a similar title but not "The Holiday" (passage [6]).

2) Ambiguities:
- Only one clear candidate in the passages for the first two films: "Rufus Sewell" (passage [4]). 
- "The Holiday" is not present in the provided passages; "Last Holiday" (passage [6]) could cause title confusion. There may be other actors who link the three films but those pages/variants are missing from the supplied passages.

3) Bridge entity / Missing fact:
- Bridge entity (as named in the passages): Rufus Sewell.
- Missing fact needed: whether Rufus Sewell (or which actor) is credited in the film "The Holiday" (i.e., confirmation of an actor who appears in all three films).

4) Retrieval direction (priority order):
- Open the "Rufus Sewell" page — check the lede/infobox and the "Filmography" / "Film" section for an entry for "The Holiday".
  Example search: "Rufus Sewell filmography The Holiday".
- Open "The Holiday (film)" page — check the Cast section for Rufus Sewell or any actor who also appears in "Hamlet" and "The Woodlanders".
  Example search: "The Holiday cast".
- If needed, open "Hamlet (1996 film)" and "The Woodlanders" film pages — check their cast lists to confirm overlapping actors and exact credited names/years.
  Example searches: "Hamlet 1996 cast", "The Woodlanders film cast".

Final answer is deferred until those pages/sections are retrieved and checked.
response.hop2_query: Which actor appeared in "Hamlet", "The Woodlanders" and "The Holiday"?
hop2_docs: Borthwood | Borthwood is a hamlet on the Isle of Wight, adjacent to Borthwood Copse, the National Trust woodland.  Borthwood includes some holiday cottages and a pet kennel.  At the 2011 Census the Post Office specified that the population of the hamlet was included in the civil parish of Newchurch, Isle of Wight.

Blissford | Blissford is a hamlet in the civil parish of Hyde situated in the New Forest National Park in Hampshire, England.  Its nearest town is Fordingbridge, which lies approximately 2 miles (2.5 km) west from the village.  The hamlet lies just to the north of the small village of Frogham.  Blissford is situated on low ground around Blissford Cross and towards Ditchend Brook where the ford can be found.  References to Blissford appear in records in the early 16th century.  The houses in the hamlet are surrounded by patches of woodland and pasture, and there is also a small settlement of mobile homes sited on higher ground.

Rufus Sewell | Rufus Frederik Sewell ( ; born 29 October 1967) is an English actor.  In film, he has appeared in Kenneth Branagh's rendition of "Hamlet" (1996) playing Fortinbras, "The Woodlanders", "Dangerous Beauty", "Dark City", "A Knight's Tale", "The Illusionist", "Tristan and Isolde", and "Martha, Meet Frank, Daniel and Laurence".  On television, he starred as "Obergruppenführer" John Smith in "The Man in the High Castle", an Italian detective in the BBC's television series "Zen" (2011) and also appeared in the mini-series "The Pillars of the Earth" (2010).  In 1993 he played the hero, Will Ladislaw, in the BBC's adaptation of George Eliot's "Middlemarch".  In 2003, he appeared in the lead role in "".  He starred in the CBS drama "Eleventh Hour", which was cancelled in April 2009.  On stage, he originated the role of Septimus Hodge in Tom Stoppard's "Arcadia" and the role of Jan in Stoppard's "Rock 'n' Roll"; the latter earned him an Olivier Award and a Tony Award nomination.

Bob Holiday | Bob Holiday (November 12, 1932 – January 27, 2017) was an American actor best known for playing Superman in the 1966 Broadway musical "It's a Bird, It's a Plane, It's Superman! " Historically, Holiday was the next "live-action" Superman after George Reeves.  Holiday played Superman more than any other actor, having played the role in over 140 performances, as well as several live appearances in character.  From 1999 until his death in 2017, he reigned as the eldest surviving, live-action Superman.

Barrow Burn | Barrowburn is a hamlet in Northumberland, England.  It comprises a working farm with associated buildings and two holiday lets.  One of the holiday lets is the Old School House, which closed as a school in the 1970's.

Center Parcs UK | Center Parcs UK is a short-break holiday company which operates five holiday villages in the United Kingdom, with each covering about 400 acre of woodland.  The company's first village opened at Sherwood Forest, Nottinghamshire, in 1987 and its fifth, at Woburn Forest, Bedfordshire, opened in 2014.
Eugene A. Eberle | Eugene A. Eberle (1840–1917) was a Broadway actor who appeared in over 60 productions, including Edwin Booth's Hamlet in 1864–65, in which he played the gravedigger.  .
